creator: swills@FreeBSD.org
text:
(In reply to John Marino from comment #3)
> assigning to ruby guru and puppet27 maintainer (who I suspect will test OP
> to change his ruby selection in make.conf and build all his packages
> manually)

Indeed, that is an option. Well, it would be, if Puppet 2.7 supported Ruby 1.9. But, Puppet 2.7 doesn't support Ruby 2.0 or newer. In fact, it doesn't even support Ruby 1.9 officially, though I didn't hear any complaints when 1.8 died, so perhaps it will work OK with 1.9 for you. See here:

https://docs.puppetlabs.com/guides/platforms.html

I probably should have noticed this and marked sysutils/puppet27 as RUBY_VER=1.8 back when we switch to Ruby 1.9 as the default version. That would have meant that once 1.9 became default, there would have been no more packages created by the regular package buildes for sysutils/puppet27 (at that time). Further, I should have marked sysutils/puppet27 deprecated and expiring when lang/ruby18 was removed. That would have killed it altogether of course. But I didn't.

It's odd to me that they list 1.8.5 and 1.8.7 as supported versions of Ruby given that the Ruby developers no longer support it, but I guess Red Hat and others are just going to keep supporting it and back porting patches when needed (and so the Puppet folks have to support the Ruby that Red Hat supports). I don't have the time to do that or to go look at what patches Red Hat or others are producing, if any, and since we generally don't do that anyway nor do we in ports support things that aren't supported upstream, axing Ruby 1.8 felt like the right thing to do.

So, I guess the answer here is just that the Puppet 2.7 port is broken with Ruby 2.x and the only thing to do is upgrade to Puppet 3.x or build your own packages with Ruby 1.9 as the default Ruby and hope it works well enough. (FWIW, the sysutils/puppet27 port is the latest version.)

If it helps, here are a few links that helped me back when I migrated to 3.x:

https://docs.puppetlabs.com/guides/install_puppet/upgrading.html

http://somethingsinistral.net/blog/the-angry-guide-to-puppet-3/

If I recall correctly, clients get updated first, then the server.

If your Puppet clients are FreeBSD and your master is on another OS and has to be older because you're getting the packages from the vendor repo which has an old 2.7 version, this might be good news. If your master is FreeBSD and your clients are another OS and you're getting the packages from the vendor repo which has old packages, this might be bad news, in which case this link might help:

https://docs.puppetlabs.com/guides/puppetlabs_package_repositories.html

If you'd rather stick with Puppet 2.7 and build your own packages, docs on setting up poudriere are here:

https://www.freebsd.org/doc/handbook/ports-poudriere.html

You'll just want to add this:

DEFAULT_VERSIONS=ruby=1.9

to /usr/local/etc/poudriere.d/make.conf. Perhaps it will work OK with 1.9 though it isn't officially supported either.

Sorry I didn't notice Puppet 2.7 breaking. Sorry you're now between a rock and a hard place and it's my fault. Let me know if I can help further, I'll do what I can to help.
